sourceTitle: Organize, manage, and track all your work with a personal dashboard

questions.question: Which command creates a to‑do block in Notion?

options.text: /to-do
options.explanation: Typing "/to-do" (or using "[ ]") inserts a checkbox to‑do block.

options.text: /toggle
options.explanation: "/toggle" creates a toggle block, not a to‑do item.

options.text: /columns
options.explanation: "/columns" creates column layouts, not a to‑do block.

options.text: /table
options.explanation: "/table" inserts a table view, which is unrelated to a to‑do block.

questions.question: Which property should you add to a tasks database to track when a task is due?

options.text: Status
options.explanation: Status tracks progress (e.g., Not started, In progress, Done).

options.text: Date
options.explanation: The Date property lets you assign and view due dates.

options.text: Select
options.explanation: Select is for custom tags like priority, not dates.

options.text: Text
options.explanation: A plain text field cannot be used for date calculations or sorting.

questions.question: Which database view is most suitable for moving tasks between statuses by dragging them?

options.text: Table view
options.explanation: Table view shows rows and columns but doesn’t support drag‑and‑drop status changes.

options.text: Calendar view
options.explanation: Calendar view visualizes dates, not status columns.

options.text: Board view
options.explanation: Board view groups tasks by a property (e.g., Status) and allows drag‑and‑drop between columns.

options.text: List view
options.explanation: List view is a simple vertical list without column‑based dragging.

questions.question: When is it generally better to start with basic blocks instead of a database for personal task tracking?

options.text: When tasks are simple, you need speed, and you mainly check items off a list.
options.explanation: Basic blocks are quick to set up and work well for straightforward checklists.

options.text: When you need to filter tasks by multiple properties such as priority and due date.
options.explanation: Filtering across properties is a strength of databases, not basic blocks.

options.text: When each task has many sub‑tasks that need their own status tracking.
options.explanation: Complex hierarchies benefit from a database structure.

options.text: When you want to visualize tasks by priority level using color‑coded columns.
options.explanation: Visual grouping by priority is easier with database views like Board or Table.

questions.question: How can you limit a linked database to show only the goals that belong to the Engineering team?

options.text: Apply a filter on the linked view to include only Engineering goals.
options.explanation: Filters applied to the linked database affect only that view, letting you surface specific records.

options.text: Edit the original company‑wide goals database to delete non‑Engineering rows.
options.explanation: Changing the source would affect everyone; filters on the linked view are the intended method.

options.text: Change the page permissions so only Engineering can see the linked database.
options.explanation: Permissions control access, not content filtering within a view.

options.text: Create separate columns for each team and move the linked database into the Engineering column.
options.explanation: Columns affect layout, not the data displayed by the linked view.

questions.question: What does the Select property enable you to do in a tasks database?

options.text: Assign a specific due date to each task.
options.explanation: Due dates are handled by the Date property.

options.text: Create a dropdown list of customizable tags such as priority or project type.
options.explanation: Select lets you define a set of options that appear in a dropdown for each record.

options.text: Track the current progress status of a task.
options.explanation: Status is a separate property designed for progress tracking.

options.text: Add free‑form text notes to a task.
options.explanation: Free‑form notes are stored in a Text property, not Select.

questions.question: Which database view would you choose to get a quick, at‑a‑glance overview of all tasks and their properties in rows and columns?

options.text: Board view
options.explanation: Board view groups tasks into columns based on a property, not rows/columns.

options.text: Calendar view
options.explanation: Calendar view focuses on dates, not a tabular layout.

options.text: Table view
options.explanation: Table view presents tasks as rows with properties as columns, ideal for a bird’s‑eye overview.

options.text: Gallery view
options.explanation: Gallery view displays cards, not a structured row/column table.

questions.question: What is the effect of editing content or properties in a linked database?

options.text: Changes are saved only in the linked copy and do not affect the original database.
options.explanation: Linked databases are live views; edits propagate to the source.

options.text: Changes are reflected in the original database, updating it for all users.
options.explanation: A linked database is a synced view; any edits affect the source database.

options.text: The linked database becomes read‑only after the first edit.
options.explanation: There is no read‑only lock; edits remain possible.

options.text: Editing creates a duplicate database that diverges from the original.
options.explanation: Duplicates are created only via explicit duplication, not by editing a linked view.

questions.question: Why might a user start with basic blocks and later convert the setup into a tasks database?

options.text: It saves time initially and adds complexity only when the workflow demands it.
options.explanation: Starting simple reduces overhead; a database can be added later if richer features are needed.

options.text: Basic blocks automatically sync with teammates, eliminating the need for a database.
options.explanation: Basic blocks are local page content; they don’t provide syncing or collaborative filtering.

options.text: Once basic blocks are used, Notion prevents you from creating a database in that page.
options.explanation: Notion allows you to add a database at any time, regardless of existing blocks.

options.text: Basic blocks include built‑in analytics that become unavailable after switching to a database.
options.explanation: Analytics are not a feature of basic blocks; they’re provided by database views and filters.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of using link previews inside a personal dashboard?

options.text: They embed static screenshots of external tools for reference.
options.explanation: Link previews are live, not static images.

options.text: They provide live, synced visualizations from external platforms, keeping information up‑to‑date.
options.explanation: Link previews continuously update, so the dashboard reflects the current state of tools like Jira or GitHub.

options.text: They replace the need for any Notion databases in a workflow.
options.explanation: Link previews complement databases; they don’t replace them.

options.text: They automatically generate new tasks in Notion whenever a linked external item changes.
options.explanation: Link previews display status but do not auto‑create Notion tasks.
